metadata.title: Britain's Secret Propaganda Department
metadata.global_visual_style: Kodachrome 60s film grain, cinematic noir lighting, historical archival photo style, high-contrast shadows, desaturated color palette with amber and slate tones. 9:16 vertical framing. Fast cuts — visual change every 3 seconds minimum.
metadata.bg_music_note: No background music. Intentional — maximizes Shorts revenue pool allocation. All audio is narration and SFX only.

timeline.narration: For 29 years, Britain secretly ran the largest propaganda operation in its history. Almost no one has heard of it.
timeline.visual_description: Black screen. Large white sans-serif text snaps in: 'BRITAIN HAD A PROPAGANDA DEPARTMENT.' Cold, stark. 9:16 vertical frame.
timeline.sfx_prompt: single typewriter keystroke, then deep silence

timeline.narration: It was called the Information Research Department — the IRD. Born in 1948, shut down in 1977. And in between: 350 covert propaganda operations worldwide.
timeline.visual_description: Animated world map, red dots appearing across Africa, Asia, the Middle East, Latin America, Europe. Each dot pulses once. Kodachrome color tones, archival paper texture overlay.
timeline.sfx_prompt: map room ambience, distant radio static

timeline.narration: The IRD invented fake organizations, forged documents, and planted fabricated stories in newspapers around the world.
timeline.visual_description: Rapid montage: extreme close-up of a forged letter with official-looking stamps; stack of newspapers in multiple languages; a hand stamping CONFIDENTIAL on a document. Fast cuts, noir lighting, heavy grain.
timeline.sfx_prompt: rubber stamp striking paper, newspaper press rolling

timeline.narration: Its cardinal rule: no material could ever identify Britain as the source. Everything had to appear completely independent.
timeline.visual_description: Interior of a 1950s intelligence office. A civil servant seals a plain brown envelope, writes a private address by hand — no departmental stamp. Tight close-up, shallow focus, noir light.
timeline.sfx_prompt: envelope sealing, pen writing, quiet office hum

timeline.narration: Journalists received IRD material at their homes — marked personal, no government reference. Most of them had no idea they were publishing state propaganda.
timeline.visual_description: A journalist's flat, London, 1952. Morning light, plain envelope being opened on a kitchen table. The journalist reads, expression unreadable. Handheld camera feel, Kodachrome grain, intimate framing.
timeline.sfx_prompt: morning ambience, paper rustling, kettle in background

timeline.narration: By the 1960s, the IRD had over 100 journalists on contact across nearly every major British newspaper.
timeline.visual_description: Wide shot of a 1960s Fleet Street newsroom. Desks crowded with typewriters, editors shouting, teletype machines chattering. Overhead angle, motion blur. Kodachrome grain, urgent atmosphere.
timeline.sfx_prompt: Fleet Street newsroom ambience — teletype clatter, shouted headlines

timeline.narration: At its peak it employed 600 staff — making it the single largest branch of the British Foreign Office.
timeline.visual_description: Wide shot of a 1960s government conference room. Rows of men in dark suits around an enormous table, documents spread out. Low-angle shot emphasizing institutional scale. Kodachrome 60s grain.
timeline.sfx_prompt: murmur of official meeting, pen scratching

timeline.narration: Here's what makes it worse. The IRD was penetrated by the KGB from day one.
timeline.visual_description: Split frame: a British newspaper front page on one side; a KGB file folder stamped in Cyrillic on the other. A gloved hand opens the KGB folder. Stark noir contrast, deep shadow, heavy grain.
timeline.sfx_prompt: file folder opening, faint Soviet-era radio broadcast

timeline.narration: Guy Burgess. Cambridge-educated. Charming. And a KGB agent since the 1930s working at the heart of the British establishment.
timeline.visual_description: Portrait photograph of Guy Burgess — well-dressed, slightly dishevelled, a knowing half-smile. Dramatic split lighting, half in shadow. Kodachrome archival photo style.
timeline.sfx_prompt: slow string tone, faint radio static

timeline.narration: Burgess was seconded to the newly-formed IRD in 1948. Within weeks, he had passed its existence, structure, and personnel list to Moscow.
timeline.visual_description: Dark government corridor, 1948. A silhouetted figure stops to light a cigarette, flame briefly illuminating his face. Echoing footsteps. Extreme noir contrast, grain-heavy.
timeline.sfx_prompt: echoing footsteps in corridor, cigarette lighter flicking

timeline.narration: Britain spent three decades running propaganda operations that the Soviets had full visibility into from the beginning.
timeline.visual_description: Side-by-side: a stack of IRD propaganda leaflets with a KGB dossier marked OPERATSIYA alongside it. A Soviet archivist's hands laid flat on both. Overhead shot, amber desk lamp. Kodachrome grain.

timeline.narration: The IRD was quietly shut down in 1977 by Foreign Secretary David Owen. No announcement. No public inquiry. Its files were sealed for decades.
timeline.visual_description: Exterior of Carlton House Terrace, London — lights going off in a upper-floor window, one by one, final closure. Overcast evening sky, Kodachrome grain, slight underexposure.
timeline.sfx_prompt: light switch click, London street ambient fading

timeline.narration: The full story is on the channel. Every operation. Every front organization. Every journalist who never knew.
timeline.visual_description: Channel-branded card — the UK Black Propaganda documentary thumbnail with bold text overlay: 'FULL STORY: 24 MINUTES'. Chronicles of Eras channel name and logo. Dark background.
LOOP SETUP — identical to opening frame: Black screen. White text: 'BRITAIN HAD A PROPAGANDA DEPARTMENT.' Holds for 4 seconds. Seamless replay trigger.
timeline.sfx_prompt: single typewriter keystroke, then deep silence
